title: Valuing Tech: Growth, Revenue Multiples & the Rule of 40
quick_definition: The standard toolkit works on a tech company — the trouble is the inputs. High-growth software runs accounting losses by design (S&M expensed today buys ARR tomorrow), so earnings multiples fail young and the analysis moves up the income statement to revenue multiples read against growth, NRR, and gross margin — with the Rule of 40 (★G302) as the sector's one-number test of whether growth is worth what it costs.
explainer_covers: Duplication guard (D4), honored first: DCF, multiples, comps, and Damodaran's companion-variable discipline are not re-taught — ER Z3.7–3.8 and ★G87–★G95 own them. This node adds only the sector bend, exactly as sector-financials Z4.1 added the bank bend (and note the symmetry: there the tools break; here the tools work but the inputs mislead).

Why earnings multiples fail young software (Damodaran, Ch. 17–20 & the money-losing/young-company chapters): reported earnings are depressed by growth spending that accounting expenses immediately, so P/E is undefined or absurd right when the business is best. The analysis moves to EV/Revenue — legitimate only with its companion variables attached: revenue growth, gross margin (★G293 — a dollar of 85%-margin revenue is worth more than a dollar of 30%-margin revenue, which is also why services firms never get software multiples, Z2.3), and NRR (★G301 — the durability of the revenue). Damodaran's young-company frame gives the intrinsic anchor: value = revenue growth × target operating margin at maturity × probability of surviving to get there.

★G302, the shorthand the sector actually talks in: revenue growth % + FCF (or operating) margin % ≥ 40. It prices the growth-vs-margin trade ER Z2.3 names in its software contrast — a company can be great growing 40% at 0% margin or growing 10% at 30% margin; what it can't be is slow and unprofitable. A screen and a language, not a valuation — the DCF discipline still governs (★G90).

The rates bend, back-linked not re-taught: ★G259 already teaches why multiples compress when rates rise; tech's twist is only that its cash flows are the market's most back-loaded, so it sits at the end of the whip — 2022 as the exhibit (growth software de-rated hardest as the risk-free rate repriced). This is also why tech is the growth style (★G149; AM z2.6's "growth dominates in low-rate, innovation-led regimes").

The other two sub-sectors, each in a sentence: semiconductors are valued as cyclicals — normalized, cycle-aware earnings, never the spot number (Z2.2; the ER Z2.3 commodity rhyme; Damodaran's cyclical caveat) — and IT services as people businesses on ordinary earnings multiples with bookings/backlog as the leading KPI (Z2.3). One sector, three valuation dialects.

connects_to.note: the opposite pathology — the sibling contrast

title: Reading a Tech 10-K: SBC, RPO & the Credit Footnote
quick_definition: Tech financials come with sector-specific traps and sector-specific gifts. The trap: stock-based compensation (★G303) — a real cost paid in shares, added back in "adjusted" numbers and quietly settled in dilution. The gift: subscription accounting leaves a paper trail (deferred revenue, RPO) that lets you see contracted future revenue no other sector discloses. And the credit footnote: for once, the standard corporate template just works.
explainer_covers: ★G303, the sector's signature adjustment: paying engineers in stock is a real expense — GAAP says so — but it's non-cash, so managements add it back to reach "adjusted" earnings and FCF. The analyst's discipline: SBC is a cost, paid by shareholders through dilution (★G42 — the concept's home; here is its industrial-scale exhibit) rather than through the cash-flow statement. Treat it as an expense (or model the share-count creep — buybacks that merely mop up SBC issuance return nothing); "FCF margin" quoted before SBC (★G92's UFCF logic, bent) flatters exactly the companies that lean on it most. At 10–25% of revenue for many software firms, this one line often decides whether a business is actually profitable.

The gift — reading the subscription paper trail (the dossier's clean filings as the lab): cash is collected upfront, revenue recognized ratably, and the difference sits as deferred revenue; RPO (remaining performance obligations — ServiceNow's and CrowdStrike's headline disclosure) is all contracted, not-yet-recognized revenue. Together they're a forward X-ray: billings and RPO growth lead reported revenue (★G192/★G193 — the KPI-before-EPS edge ER teaches, in its purest sector form). Watch RPO decelerate before revenue ever does.

The R&D question (Damodaran's adjustment, flagged not drilled): accounting expenses R&D, but it's really investment in intangible assets — so tech book values are meaningless (why P/B, the Financials chart, is a dead letter here) and reported margins understate the mature-state economics. Capitalizing R&D is the adjustment; the depth stays with Damodaran/ER.

The credit footnote — the FI Z4.8 handshake, completed as a back-link: judging a tech company as a borrower uses the standard template FI already taught — coverage (★G74), leverage (★G29), covenants (★G34) all apply cleanly; recurring revenue actually makes the template work unusually well (predictable EBITDA is what lenders price — the reason software became modern private credit's and PE's favorite borrower, and lenders now underwrite pre-profit software on ARR itself). The sector wrinkles fit in two sentences: the IG end is the paradox of cash-rich mega-caps issuing bonds anyway (★G274–★G277 for the market's read — Apple's FY2025 10-K shows $91.3B of notes outstanding alongside $132.4B of cash and marketable securities), and the HY/leveraged end is the software LBO. That second phenomenon — ARR-based lending — has no home in the Private Credit map yet; seam flagged rather than taught out of place here.

What the analyst's dashboard looks like assembled (the zone's send-off): growth + NRR + gross margin (Z3.2) → Rule of 40 (Z4.1) → SBC-honest FCF and RPO trajectory (this node) → a view. Then the door back out: the full research craft — models, estimates, the published call — is Equity Research's (★G183/★G184), reached through the Z1.1 door.
